evidence_dialogues.utterance: Don't give in, Thomas. I will tell the boys to go with you.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
evidence_dialogues.utterance: Morten will be delighted; and Ejlif will do whatever he does.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
evidence_dialogues.utterance: Yes, and Petra!—and you too, Katherine!

reasoning_steps.inference: Mrs. Stockmann identifies Morten and Ejlif as "the boys" who will go with Thomas.
Dr. Stockmann adds Petra to this family unit, establishing Morten and Ejlif as her siblings. Thus, 2 siblings are named.

qa.question: Which phrase best encapsulates the printer's overarching political and professional philosophy?
option: A. Radical revolution at all costs.
B. Timid moderation and protection of local taxpayers' financial interests.
C. Unwavering loyalty to the Medical Officer's scientific discoveries.
D. He absolutely believes that the local newspaper should be immediately shut down by the government to prevent the spread of dangerous liberal ideologies among the working class.
E. Aggressive pursuit of higher office.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Aslaksen
evidence_dialogues.utterance: Timid? Yes, when it is a question of the local authorities, I am timid... But local authorities are different; they can be turned out, and then perhaps you may get an ignorant lot into office who may do irreparable harm to the householders

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Aslaksen
evidence_dialogues.utterance: And if only we proceed with moderation, I cannot imagine that there will be any risk.

reasoning_steps.inference: He admits to being timid regarding local authorities to protect householders.
He constantly preaches moderation to avoid risk.

qa.question: What is the printer's immediate reaction upon hearing the actual cost of the proposed municipal project?
option: A. He immediately offers to pay for it.
B. He panics over the prospect of the small tradesmen having to foot the bill.
C. He calmly agrees it is a necessary expense.
D. He completely ignores the Mayor's financial estimate and instead asks the editor for a raise in his weekly salary.
E. He laughs at the Mayor's exaggeration.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Peter Stockmann
evidence_dialogues.utterance: At a provisional estimate, the alterations that the Medical Officer asserts to be desirable will cost somewhere about twenty thousand pounds.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Aslaksen
evidence_dialogues.utterance: Do you mean that it must come out of the municipal funds?—out of the ill-filled pockets of the small tradesmen?

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Aslaksen
evidence_dialogues.utterance: The gentlemen who own the Baths ought to provide that.

reasoning_steps.inference: Upon hearing the cost, he immediately worries it will come from the municipal funds.
He anxiously attempts to shift the burden to the wealthy Bath owners to protect the small tradesmen.

option: A. He refuses it, then accepts it.
B. He initially promises to handle it like a treasure, but later outright refuses to print it at any price.
C. He ignores it initially, then burns it.
D. He enthusiastically begins typesetting it in the back room, but eventually abandons the project when he realizes he has run out of black ink.
E. He is consistently opposed to it.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
evidence_dialogues.utterance: What I wanted to ask you, Mr. Aslaksen, was if you would supervise the printing of it yourself.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Aslaksen
evidence_dialogues.utterance: With pleasure.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Aslaksen
evidence_dialogues.utterance: If you offered me its weight in gold, I could not lend my press for any such purpose, Doctor. It would be flying in the face of public opinion.

reasoning_steps.inference: He initially accepts the task of supervising the printing with pleasure.
Later, he vehemently refuses to lend his press even for its weight in gold.

option: A. He argues for an increase in taxes.
B. He argues that the wealthy gentlemen who own the Baths should be the ones to provide the money.
C. He argues that the national government should intervene.
D. He vehemently argues that the Medical Officer should be forced to personally pay for all of the damages out of his own pocket.
E. He argues that they should borrow money from the bank.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Peter Stockmann
evidence_dialogues.utterance: Well, my dear Mr. Aslaksen, where else is the money to come from?

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Aslaksen
evidence_dialogues.utterance: The gentlemen who own the Baths ought to provide that.

reasoning_steps.inference: The Mayor asks where the money should come from.
Aslaksen directly counters that the gentlemen owners should provide it.

qa.question: According to the text, what specific community group does the printer claim to have considerable influence among?
option: A. The large shareholders.
B. The small tradesmen.
C. The local aristocracy.
D. He claims to be the absolute leader of the town's secret underground revolutionary society, which primarily consists of disgruntled former naval officers.
E. The medical board.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Peter Stockmann
evidence_dialogues.utterance: And a man of very considerable influence.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Aslaksen
evidence_dialogues.utterance: Chiefly among the small tradesmen, sir.

reasoning_steps.inference: The Mayor remarks on his influence.
Aslaksen specifies that his influence is chiefly among the small tradesmen.

option: A. One year.
B. Two years.
C. Three months.
D. He repeats in utter disbelief that the facilities will be completely shut down for exactly five decades.
E. Six weeks.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Peter Stockmann
evidence_dialogues.utterance: The most fatal part of it is that we shall be obliged to shut the Baths for a couple of years.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Aslaksen
evidence_dialogues.utterance: For two years?

reasoning_steps.inference: The Mayor mentions shutting the Baths for a couple of years.
Aslaksen repeats "For two years?" to express his shock.

session_3.speaker: NARRATION
session_3.text: (SCENE.—The editorial office of the "People's Messenger." The entrance door is on the left-hand side of the back wall; on the right-hand side is another door with glass panels through which the printing room can be seen. Another door in the right-hand wall. In the middle of the room is a large table covered with papers, newspapers and books. In the foreground on the left a window, before which stands a desk and a high stool. There are a couple of easy chairs by the table, and other chairs standing along the wall. The room is dingy and uncomfortable; the furniture is old, the chairs stained and torn. In the printing room the compositors are seen at work, and a printer is working a handpress. HOVSTAD is sitting at the desk, writing. BILLING comes in from the right with DR. STOCKMANN'S manuscript in his hand.)

session_3.speaker: Billing
session_3.text: Well, I must say!

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Have you read it through?

session_3.speaker: Billing
session_3.text: Yes, indeed I have.

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Don't you think the Doctor hits them pretty hard?

session_3.speaker: Billing
session_3.text: Hard? Bless my soul, he's crushing! Every word falls like—how shall I put it?—like the blow of a sledgehammer.

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Yes, but they are not the people to throw up the sponge at the first blow.

session_3.speaker: Billing
session_3.text: That is true; and for that reason we must strike blow upon blow until the whole of this aristocracy tumbles to pieces. As I sat in there reading this, I almost seemed to see a revolution in being.

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Hush!—Speak so that Aslaksen cannot hear you.

session_3.speaker: Billing
session_3.text: Aslaksen is a chicken-hearted chap, a coward; there is nothing of the man in him. But this time you will insist on your own way, won't you? You will put the Doctor's article in?

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Yes, and if the Mayor doesn't like it—

session_3.speaker: Billing
session_3.text: That will be the devil of a nuisance.

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Well, fortunately we can turn the situation to good account, whatever happens. If the Mayor will not fall in with the Doctor's project, he will have all the small tradesmen down on him—the whole of the Householders' Association and the rest of them. And if he does fall in with it, he will fall out with the whole crowd of large shareholders in the Baths, who up to now have been his most valuable supporters—

session_3.speaker: Billing
session_3.text: Yes, because they will certainly have to fork out a pretty penny—

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Yes, you may be sure they will. And in this way the ring will be broken up, you see, and then in every issue of the paper we will enlighten the public on the Mayor's incapability on one point and another, and make it clear that all the positions of trust in the town, the whole control of municipal affairs, ought to be put in the hands of the Liberals.

session_3.speaker: Billing
session_3.text: That is perfectly true! I see it coming—I see it coming; we are on the threshold of a revolution!

session_3.speaker: STAGE
session_3.text: (A knock is heard at the door.)

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Hush! Come in! Ah, it is you, Doctor! Well?

session_3.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_3.text: You may set to work and print it, Mr. Hovstad!

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Has it come to that, then?

session_3.speaker: Billing
session_3.text: Hurrah!

session_3.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_3.text: Yes, print away. Undoubtedly it has come to that. Now they must take what they get. There is going to be a fight in the town, Mr. Billing!

session_3.speaker: Billing
session_3.text: War to the knife, I hope! We will get our knives to their throats, Doctor!

session_3.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_3.text: This article is only a beginning. I have already got four or five more sketched out in my head. Where is Aslaksen?

session_3.speaker: Billing
session_3.text: Aslaksen, just come here for a minute!

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Four or five more articles, did you say? On the same subject?

session_3.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_3.text: No—far from it, my dear fellow. No, they are about quite another matter. But they all spring from the question of the water supply and the drainage. One thing leads to another, you know. It is like beginning to pull down an old house, exactly.

session_3.speaker: Billing
session_3.text: Upon my soul, it's true; you find you are not done till you have pulled all the old rubbish down.

session_3.speaker: Aslaksen
session_3.text: Pulled down? You are not thinking of pulling down the Baths surely, Doctor?

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Far from it, don't be afraid.

session_3.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_3.text: No, we meant something quite different. Well, what do you think of my article, Mr. Hovstad?

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: I think it is simply a masterpiece.

session_3.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_3.text: Do you really think so? Well, I am very pleased, very pleased.

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: It is so clear and intelligible. One need have no special knowledge to understand the bearing of it. You will have every enlightened man on your side.

session_3.speaker: Aslaksen
session_3.text: And every prudent man too, I hope?

session_3.speaker: Billing
session_3.text: The prudent and the imprudent—almost the whole town.

session_3.speaker: Aslaksen
session_3.text: In that case we may venture to print it.

session_3.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_3.text: I should think so!

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: We will put it in tomorrow morning.

session_3.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_3.text: Of course—you must not lose a single day. What I wanted to ask you, Mr. Aslaksen, was if you would supervise the printing of it yourself.

session_3.speaker: Aslaksen
session_3.text: With pleasure.

session_3.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_3.text: Take care of it as if it were a treasure! No misprints—every word is important. I will look in again a little later; perhaps you will be able to let me see a proof. I can't tell you how eager I am to see it in print, and see it burst upon the public—

session_3.speaker: Billing
session_3.text: Burst upon them—yes, like a flash of lightning!

session_3.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_3.text: —and to have it submitted to the judgment of my intelligent fellow townsmen. You cannot imagine what I have gone through today. I have been threatened first with one thing and then with another; they have tried to rob me of my most elementary rights as a man—

session_3.speaker: Billing
session_3.text: What! Your rights as a man!

session_3.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_3.text: they have tried to degrade me, to make a coward of me, to force me to put personal interests before my most sacred convictions.

session_3.speaker: Billing
session_3.text: That is too much—I'm damned if it isn't.

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Oh, you mustn't be surprised at anything from that quarter.

session_3.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_3.text: Well, they will get the worst of it with me; they may assure themselves of that. I shall consider the "People's Messenger" my sheet-anchor now, and every single day I will bombard them with one article after another, like bombshells—

session_3.speaker: Aslaksen
session_3.text: Yes, but

session_3.speaker: Billing
session_3.text: Hurrah!—it is war, it is war!

session_3.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_3.text: I shall smite them to the ground—I shall crush them—I shall break down all their defenses, before the eyes of the honest public! That is what I shall do!

session_3.speaker: Aslaksen
session_3.text: Yes, but in moderation, Doctor—proceed with moderation.

session_3.speaker: Billing
session_3.text: Not a bit of it, not a bit of it! Don't spare the dynamite!

session_3.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_3.text: Because it is not merely a question of water-supply and drains now, you know. No—it is the whole of our social life that we have got to purify and disinfect—

session_3.speaker: Billing
session_3.text: Spoken like a deliverer!

session_3.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_3.text: All the incapables must be turned out, you understand—and that in every walk of life! Endless vistas have opened themselves to my mind's eye today. I cannot see it all quite clearly yet, but I shall in time. Young and vigorous standard-bearers—those are what we need and must seek, my friends; we must have new men in command at all our outposts.

session_3.speaker: Billing
session_3.text: Hear hear!

session_3.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_3.text: We only need to stand by one another, and it will all be perfectly easy. The revolution will be launched like a ship that runs smoothly off the stocks. Don't you think so?

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: For my part I think we have now a prospect of getting the municipal authority into the hands where it should lie.

session_3.speaker: Aslaksen
session_3.text: And if only we proceed with moderation, I cannot imagine that there will be any risk.

session_3.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_3.text: Who the devil cares whether there is any risk or not! What I am doing, I am doing in the name of truth and for the sake of my conscience.

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: You are a man who deserves to be supported, Doctor.

session_3.speaker: Aslaksen
session_3.text: Yes, there is no denying that the Doctor is a true friend to the town—a real friend to the community, that he is.

session_3.speaker: Billing
session_3.text: Take my word for it, Aslaksen, Dr. Stockmann is a friend of the people.

session_3.speaker: Aslaksen
session_3.text: I fancy the Householders' Association will make use of that expression before long.

session_3.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_3.text: Thank you, thank you, my dear staunch friends. It is very refreshing to me to hear you say that; my brother called me something quite different. By Jove, he shall have it back, with interest! But now I must be off to see a poor devil—I will come back, as I said. Keep a very careful eye on the manuscript, Aslaksen, and don't for worlds leave out any of my notes of exclamation! Rather put one or two more in! Capital, capital! Well, good-bye for the present—goodbye, goodbye!

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: He may prove an invaluably useful man to us.

session_3.speaker: Aslaksen
session_3.text: Yes, so long as he confines himself to this matter of the Baths. But if he goes farther afield, I don't think it would be advisable to follow him.

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Hm!—that all depends—

session_3.speaker: Billing
session_3.text: You are so infernally timid, Aslaksen!

session_3.speaker: Aslaksen
session_3.text: Timid? Yes, when it is a question of the local authorities, I am timid, Mr. Billing; it is a lesson I have learned in the school of experience, let me tell you. But try me in higher politics, in matters that concern the government itself, and then see if I am timid.

session_3.speaker: Billing
session_3.text: No, you aren't, I admit. But this is simply contradicting yourself.

session_3.speaker: Aslaksen
session_3.text: I am a man with a conscience, and that is the whole matter. If you attack the government, you don't do the community any harm, anyway; those fellows pay no attention to attacks, you see—they go on just as they are, in spite of them. But local authorities are different; they can be turned out, and then perhaps you may get an ignorant lot into office who may do irreparable harm to the householders and everybody else.

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: But what of the education of citizens by self government—don't you attach any importance to that?

session_3.speaker: Aslaksen
session_3.text: When a man has interests of his own to protect, he cannot think of everything, Mr. Hovstad.

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Then I hope I shall never have interests of my own to protect!

session_3.speaker: Billing
session_3.text: Hear, hear!

session_3.speaker: Aslaksen
session_3.text: Hm! Mr. Sheriff Stensgaard was your predecessor at that editorial desk.

session_3.speaker: Billing
session_3.text: Bah! That turncoat.

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: I am not a weathercock—and never will be.

session_3.speaker: Aslaksen
session_3.text: A politician should never be too certain of anything, Mr. Hovstad. And as for you, Mr. Billing, I should think it is time for you to be taking in a reef or two in your sails, seeing that you are applying for the post of secretary to the Bench.

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Are you, Billing?

session_3.speaker: Billing
session_3.text: Well, yes—but you must clearly understand I am only doing it to annoy the bigwigs.

session_3.speaker: Aslaksen
session_3.text: Anyhow, it is no business of mine. But if I am to be accused of timidity and of inconsistency in my principles, this is what I want to point out: my political past is an open book. I have never changed, except perhaps to become a little more moderate, you see. My heart is still with the people; but I don't deny that my reason has a certain bias towards the authorities—the local ones, I mean.

session_3.speaker: Billing
session_3.text: Oughtn't we to try and get rid of him, Hovstad?

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Do you know anyone else who will advance the money for our paper and printing bill?

session_3.speaker: Billing
session_3.text: It is an infernal nuisance that we don't possess some capital to trade on.

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Yes, if we only had that, then—

session_3.speaker: Billing
session_3.text: Suppose you were to apply to Dr. Stockmann?

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: What is the use? He has got nothing.

session_3.speaker: Billing
session_3.text: No, but he has got a warm man in the background, old Morten Kiil—"the Badger," as they call him.

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Are you so sure he has got anything?

session_3.speaker: Billing
session_3.text: Good Lord, of course he has! And some of it must come to the Stockmanns. Most probably he will do something for the children, at all events.

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Are you counting on that?

session_3.speaker: Billing
session_3.text: Counting on it? Of course I am not counting on anything.

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: That is right. And I should not count on the secretaryship to the Bench either, if I were you; for I can assure you—you won't get it.

session_3.speaker: Billing
session_3.text: Do you think I am not quite aware of that? My object is precisely not to get it. A slight of that kind stimulates a man's fighting power—it is like getting a supply of fresh bile—and I am sure one needs that badly enough in a hole-and-corner place like this, where it is so seldom anything happens to stir one up.

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Quite so, quite so.

session_3.speaker: Billing
session_3.text: Ah, I shall be heard of yet!—Now I shall go and write the appeal to the Householders' Association.

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Hm!—that's it, is it. Come in! What, you!—here?

session_3.speaker: Petra
session_3.text: Yes, you must forgive me—

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Won't you sit down?

session_3.speaker: Petra
session_3.text: No, thank you; I must go again in a moment.

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Have you come with a message from your father, by any chance?

session_3.speaker: Petra
session_3.text: No, I have come on my own account. Here is the English story.

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Why have you brought it back?

session_3.speaker: Petra
session_3.text: Because I am not going to translate it.

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: But you promised me faithfully.

session_3.speaker: Petra
session_3.text: Yes, but then I had not read it, I don't suppose you have read it either?

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: No, you know quite well I don't understand English; but—

session_3.speaker: Petra
session_3.text: Quite so. That is why I wanted to tell you that you must find something else. You can't use this for the "People's Messenger."

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Why not?

session_3.speaker: Petra
session_3.text: Because it conflicts with all your opinions.

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Oh, for that matter—

session_3.speaker: Petra
session_3.text: You don't understand me. The burden of this story is that there is a supernatural power that looks after the so-called good people in this world and makes everything happen for the best in their case—while all the so-called bad people are punished.

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Well, but that is all right. That is just what our readers want.

session_3.speaker: Petra
session_3.text: And are you going to be the one to give it to them? For myself, I do not believe a word of it. You know quite well that things do not happen so in reality.

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: You are perfectly right; but an editor cannot always act as he would prefer. He is often obliged to bow to the wishes of the public in unimportant matters. Politics are the most important thing in life—for a newspaper, anyway; and if I want to carry my public with me on the path that leads to liberty and progress, I must not frighten them away. If they find a moral tale of this sort in the serial at the bottom of the page, they will be all the more ready to read what is printed above it; they feel more secure, as it were.

session_3.speaker: Petra
session_3.text: For shame! You would never go and set a snare like that for your readers; you are not a spider!

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Thank you for having such a good opinion of me. No; as a matter of fact that is Billing's idea and not mine.

session_3.speaker: Petra
session_3.text: Billing's!

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Yes; anyway, he propounded that theory here one day. And it is Billing who is so anxious to have that story in the paper; I don't know anything about the book.

session_3.speaker: Petra
session_3.text: But how can Billing, with his emancipated views—

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Oh, Billing is a many-sided man. He is applying for the post of secretary to the Bench, too, I hear.

session_3.speaker: Petra
session_3.text: I don't believe it, Mr. Hovstad. How could he possibly bring himself to do such a thing?

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Ah, you must ask him that.

session_3.speaker: Petra
session_3.text: I should never have thought it of him.

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: No? Does it really surprise you so much?

session_3.speaker: Petra
session_3.text: Yes. Or perhaps not altogether. Really, I don't quite know

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: We journalists are not much worth, Miss Stockmann.

session_3.speaker: Petra
session_3.text: Do you really mean that?

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: I think so sometimes.

session_3.speaker: Petra
session_3.text: Yes, in the ordinary affairs of everyday life, perhaps; I can understand that. But now, when you have taken a weighty matter in hand—

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: This matter of your father's, you mean?

session_3.speaker: Petra
session_3.text: Exactly. It seems to me that now you must feel you are a man worth more than most.

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Yes, today I do feel something of that sort.

session_3.speaker: Petra
session_3.text: Of course you do, don't you? It is a splendid vocation you have chosen—to smooth the way for the march of unappreciated truths, and new and courageous lines of thought. If it were nothing more than because you stand fearlessly in the open and take up the cause of an injured man—

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Especially when that injured man is—ahem!—I don't rightly know how to—

session_3.speaker: Petra
session_3.text: When that man is so upright and so honest, you mean?

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Especially when he is your father I meant.

session_3.speaker: Petra
session_3.text: That?

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Yes, Petra—Miss Petra.

session_3.speaker: Petra
session_3.text: Is it that, that is first and foremost with you? Not the matter itself? Not the truth?—not my father's big generous heart?

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Certainly—of course—that too.

session_3.speaker: Petra
session_3.text: No, thank you; you have betrayed yourself, Mr. Hovstad, and now I shall never trust you again in anything.

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Can you really take it so amiss in me that it is mostly for your sake—?

session_3.speaker: Petra
session_3.text: What I am angry with you for, is for not having been honest with my father. You talked to him as if the truth and the good of the community were what lay nearest to your heart. You have made fools of both my father and me. You are not the man you made yourself out to be. And that I shall never forgive you-never!

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: You ought not to speak so bitterly, Miss Petra—least of all now.

session_3.speaker: Petra
session_3.text: Why not now, especially?

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Because your father cannot do without my help.

session_3.speaker: Petra
session_3.text: Are you that sort of man too? For shame!

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: No, no, I am not. This came upon me so unexpectedly—you must believe that.

session_3.speaker: Petra
session_3.text: I know what to believe. Goodbye.

session_3.speaker: Aslaksen
session_3.text: Damnation, Hovstad!—Oh, this is awkward—

session_3.speaker: Petra
session_3.text: There is the book; you must give it to some one else.

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: But, Miss Stockmann—

session_3.speaker: Petra
session_3.text: Goodbye.

session_3.speaker: Aslaksen
session_3.text: I say—Mr. Hovstad—

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Well well!—what is it?

session_3.speaker: Aslaksen
session_3.text: The Mayor is outside in the printing room.

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: The Mayor, did you say?

session_3.speaker: Aslaksen
session_3.text: Yes he wants to speak to you. He came in by the back door—didn't want to be seen, you understand.

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: What can he want? Wait a bit—I will go myself.

session_3.speaker: Aslaksen
session_3.text: Quite so.

session_3.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_3.text: You did not expect to see me here, Mr. Hovstad?

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: No, I confess I did not.

session_3.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_3.text: You are very snug in here—very nice indeed.

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Oh—

session_3.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_3.text: And here I come, without any notice, to take up your time!

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: By all means, Mr. Mayor. I am at your service. But let me relieve you of your—Won't you sit down?

session_3.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_3.text: Thank you. I have had an extremely annoying experience to-day, Mr. Hovstad.

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Really? Ah well, I expect with all the various business you have to attend to—

session_3.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_3.text: The Medical Officer of the Baths is responsible for what happened today.

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Indeed? The Doctor?

session_3.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_3.text: He has addressed a kind of report to the Baths Committee on the subject of certain supposed defects in the Baths.

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Has he indeed?

session_3.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_3.text: Yes—has he not told you? I thought he said—

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Ah, yes—it is true he did mention something about—

session_3.speaker: Aslaksen
session_3.text: I ought to have that copy.

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Ahem!—there it is on the desk.

session_3.speaker: Aslaksen
session_3.text: Right.

session_3.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_3.text: But look there—that is the thing I was speaking of!

session_3.speaker: Aslaksen
session_3.text: Yes, that is the Doctor's article, Mr. Mayor.

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Oh, is THAT what you were speaking about?

session_3.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_3.text: Yes, that is it. What do you think of it?

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Oh, I am only a layman—and I have only taken a very cursory glance at it.

session_3.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_3.text: But you are going to print it?

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: I cannot very well refuse a distinguished man.

session_3.speaker: Aslaksen
session_3.text: I have nothing to do with editing the paper, Mr. Mayor—

session_3.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_3.text: I understand.

session_3.speaker: Aslaksen
session_3.text: I merely print what is put into my hands.

session_3.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_3.text: Quite so.

session_3.speaker: Aslaksen
session_3.text: And so I must—

session_3.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_3.text: No, but wait a moment, Mr. Aslaksen. You will allow me, Mr. Hovstad?

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: If you please, Mr. Mayor.

session_3.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_3.text: You are a discreet and thoughtful man, Mr. Aslaksen.

session_3.speaker: Aslaksen
session_3.text: I am delighted to hear you think so, sir.

session_3.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_3.text: And a man of very considerable influence.

session_3.speaker: Aslaksen
session_3.text: Chiefly among the small tradesmen, sir.

session_3.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_3.text: The small tax-payers are the majority—here as everywhere else.

session_3.speaker: Aslaksen
session_3.text: That is true.

session_3.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_3.text: And I have no doubt you know the general trend of opinion among them, don't you?

session_3.speaker: Aslaksen
session_3.text: Yes I think I may say I do, Mr. Mayor.

session_3.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_3.text: Yes. Well, since there is such a praiseworthy spirit of self-sacrifice among the less wealthy citizens of our town—

session_3.speaker: Aslaksen
session_3.text: What?

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Self-sacrifice?

session_3.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_3.text: It is pleasing evidence of a public-spirited feeling, extremely pleasing evidence. I might almost say I hardly expected it. But you have a closer knowledge of public opinion than I.

session_3.speaker: Aslaksen
session_3.text: But, Mr. Mayor—

session_3.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_3.text: And indeed it is no small sacrifice that the town is going to make.

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: The town?

session_3.speaker: Aslaksen
session_3.text: But I don't understand. Is it the Baths—?

session_3.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_3.text: At a provisional estimate, the alterations that the Medical Officer asserts to be desirable will cost somewhere about twenty thousand pounds.

session_3.speaker: Aslaksen
session_3.text: That is a lot of money, but—

session_3.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_3.text: Of course it will be necessary to raise a municipal loan.

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Surely you never mean that the town must pay—?

session_3.speaker: Aslaksen
session_3.text: Do you mean that it must come out of the municipal funds?—out of the ill-filled pockets of the small tradesmen?

session_3.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_3.text: Well, my dear Mr. Aslaksen, where else is the money to come from?

session_3.speaker: Aslaksen
session_3.text: The gentlemen who own the Baths ought to provide that.

session_3.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_3.text: The proprietors of the Baths are not in a position to incur any further expense.

session_3.speaker: Aslaksen
session_3.text: Is that absolutely certain, Mr. Mayor?

session_3.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_3.text: I have satisfied myself that it is so. If the town wants these very extensive alterations, it will have to pay for them.

session_3.speaker: Aslaksen
session_3.text: But, damn it all—I beg your pardon—this is quite another matter, Mr. Hovstad!

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: It is, indeed.

session_3.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_3.text: The most fatal part of it is that we shall be obliged to shut the Baths for a couple of years.

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Shut them? Shut them altogether?

session_3.speaker: Aslaksen
session_3.text: For two years?

session_3.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_3.text: Yes, the work will take as long as that—at least.

session_3.speaker: Aslaksen
session_3.text: I'm damned if we will stand that, Mr. Mayor! What are we householder to live upon in the meantime?

session_3.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_3.text: Unfortunately, that is an extremely difficult question to answer, Mr. Aslaksen. But what would you have us do? Do you suppose we shall have a single visitor in the town, if we go about proclaiming that our water is polluted, that we are living over a plague spot, that the entire town—

session_3.speaker: Aslaksen
session_3.text: And the whole thing is merely imagination?

session_3.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_3.text: With the best will in the world, I have not been able to come to any other conclusion.

session_3.speaker: Aslaksen
session_3.text: Well then I must say it is absolutely unjustifiable of Dr. Stockmann—I beg your pardon, Mr. Mayor.

session_3.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_3.text: What you say is lamentably true, Mr. Aslaksen. My brother has unfortunately always been a headstrong man.

session_3.speaker: Aslaksen
session_3.text: After this, do you mean to give him your support, Mr. Hovstad?

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Can you suppose for a moment that I—?

session_3.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_3.text: I have drawn up a short resume of the situation as it appears from a reasonable man's point of view. In it I have indicated how certain possible defects might suitably be remedied without outrunning the resources of the Baths Committee.

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Have you got it with you, Mr. Mayor?

session_3.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_3.text: Yes, I brought it with me in case you should—

session_3.speaker: Aslaksen
session_3.text: Good Lord, there he is!

session_3.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_3.text: Who? My brother?

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Where? Where?

session_3.speaker: Aslaksen
session_3.text: He has just gone through the printing room.

session_3.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_3.text: How unlucky! I don't want to meet him here, and I had still several things to speak to you about.

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Go in there for the present.

session_3.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_3.text: But—?

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: You will only find Billing in there.

session_3.speaker: Aslaksen
session_3.text: Quick, quick, Mr. Mayor—he is just coming.

session_3.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_3.text: Yes, very well; but see that you get rid of him quickly.

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Pretend to be doing something, Aslaksen.

session_3.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_3.text: Here I am again.

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Already, Doctor? Hurry up with what we were speaking about, Aslaksen. We are very pressed for time today.

session_3.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_3.text: No proof for me to see yet, I hear.

session_3.speaker: Aslaksen
session_3.text: You couldn't expect it yet, Doctor.

session_3.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_3.text: No, no; but I am impatient, as you can understand. I shall not know a moment's peace of mind until I see it in print.

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Hm!—It will take a good while yet, won't it, Aslaksen?

session_3.speaker: Aslaksen
session_3.text: Yes, I am almost afraid it will.

session_3.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_3.text: All right, my dear friends; I will come back. I do not mind coming back twice if necessary. A matter of such great importance—the welfare of the town at stake—it is no time to shirk trouble, Look here—there is one thing more I want to speak to you about.

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Excuse me, but could it not wait till some other time?

session_3.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_3.text: I can tell you in half a dozen words. It is only this.

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Yes but, Doctor—

session_3.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_3.text: I know what you are going to say. You don't see how on earth it was any more than my duty—my obvious duty as a citizen. Of course it wasn't; I know that as well as you. But my fellow citizens, you know—! Good Lord, think of all the good souls who think so highly of me—!

session_3.speaker: Aslaksen
session_3.text: Yes, our townsfolk have had a very high opinion of you so far, Doctor.

session_3.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_3.text: Yes, and that is just why I am afraid they—. Well, this is the point; when this reaches them, especially the poorer classes, and sounds in their ears like a summons to take the town's affairs into their own hands for the future...

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Ahem! Doctor, I won't conceal from you the fact—

session_3.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_3.text: Ah I—I knew there was something in the wind! But I won't hear a word of it. If anything of that sort is being set on foot—

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Of what sort?

session_3.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_3.text: Well, whatever it is—whether it is a demonstration in my honour, or a banquet, or a subscription list for some presentation to me—whatever it is, you most promise me solemnly and faithfully to put a stop to it. You too, Mr. Aslaksen; do you understand?

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: You must forgive me, Doctor, but sooner or later we must tell you the plain truth—

session_3.speaker: STAGE
session_3.text: (He is interrupted by the entrance Of MRS. STOCKMANN, who comes in from the street door.)

session_3.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_3.text: Just as I thought!

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: You too, Mrs. Stockmann?

session_3.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_3.text: What on earth do you want here, Katherine?

session_3.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_3.text: I should think you know very well what I want.

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Won't you sit down? Or perhaps—

session_3.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_3.text: No, thank you; don't trouble. And you must not be offended at my coming to fetch my husband; I am the mother of three children, you know.

session_3.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_3.text: Nonsense!—we know all about that.

session_3.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_3.text: Well, one would not give you credit for much thought for your wife and children today; if you had had that, you would not have gone and dragged us all into misfortune.

session_3.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_3.text: Are you out of your senses, Katherine! Because a man has a wife and children, is he not to be allowed to proclaim the truth-is he not to be allowed to be an actively useful citizen—is he not to be allowed to do a service to his native town!

session_3.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_3.text: Yes, Thomas—in reason.

session_3.speaker: Aslaksen
session_3.text: Just what I say. Moderation in everything.

session_3.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_3.text: And that is why you wrong us, Mr. Hovstad, in enticing my husband away from his home and making a dupe of him in all this.

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: I certainly am making a dupe of no one—

session_3.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_3.text: Making a dupe of me! Do you suppose I should allow myself to be duped!

session_3.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_3.text: It is just what you do. I know quite well you have more brains than anyone in the town, but you are extremely easily duped, Thomas. Please do realise that he loses his post at the Baths if you print what he has written.

session_3.speaker: Aslaksen
session_3.text: What!

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Look here, Doctor!

session_3.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_3.text: Ha-ha!—just let them try! No, no—they will take good care not to. I have got the compact majority behind me, let me tell you!

session_3.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_3.text: Yes, that is just the worst of it—your having any such horrid thing behind you.

session_3.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_3.text: Rubbish, Katherine!—Go home and look after your house and leave me to look after the community. How can you be so afraid, when I am so confident and happy? Truth and the People will win the fight, you may be certain! I see the whole of the broad-minded middle class marching like a victorious army—! What the deuce is that lying there?

session_3.speaker: Aslaksen
session_3.text: Good Lord!

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Ahem!

session_3.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_3.text: Here we have the topmost pinnacle of authority!

session_3.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_3.text: The Mayor's hat!

session_3.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_3.text: And here is the staff of office too. How in the name of all that's wonderful—?

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Well, you see—

session_3.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_3.text: Oh, I understand. He has been here trying to talk you over. Ha-ha!—he made rather a mistake there! And as soon as he caught sight of me in the printing room. Did he run away, Mr. Aslaksen?

session_3.speaker: Aslaksen
session_3.text: Yes, he ran away, Doctor.

session_3.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_3.text: Ran away without his stick or his—. Fiddlesticks! Peter doesn't run away and leave his belongings behind him. But what the deuce have you done with him? Ah!—in there, of course. Now you shall see, Katherine!

session_3.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_3.text: Thomas—please don't—!

session_3.speaker: Aslaksen
session_3.text: Don't be rash, Doctor.

session_3.speaker: STAGE
session_3.text: (DR. STOCKMANN has put on the Mayor's hat and taken his stick in his hand. He goes up to the door, opens it, and stands with his hand to his hat at the salute. PETER STOCKMANN comes in, red with anger. BILLING follows him.)

session_3.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_3.text: What does this tomfoolery mean?

session_3.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_3.text: Be respectful, my good Peter. I am the chief authority in the town now.

session_3.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_3.text: Really, Thomas!

session_3.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_3.text: Give me my hat and stick.

session_3.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_3.text: If you are chief constable, let me tell you that I am the Mayor—I am the master of the whole town, please understand!

session_3.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_3.text: Take off my hat, I tell you. Remember it is part of an official uniform.

session_3.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_3.text: Pooh! Do you think the newly awakened lionhearted people are going to be frightened by an official hat? There is going to be a revolution in the town tomorrow, let me tell you. You thought you could turn me out; but now I shall turn you out—turn you out of all your various offices. Do you think I cannot? Listen to me. I have triumphant social forces behind me. Hovstad and Billing will thunder in the "People's Messenger," and Aslaksen will take the field at the head of the whole Householders' Association—

session_3.speaker: Aslaksen
session_3.text: That I won't, Doctor.

session_3.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_3.text: Of course you will—

session_3.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_3.text: Ah!—may I ask then if Mr. Hovstad intends to join this agitation?

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: No, Mr. Mayor.

session_3.speaker: Aslaksen
session_3.text: No, Mr. Hovstad is not such a fool as to go and ruin his paper and himself for the sake of an imaginary grievance.

session_3.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_3.text: What does this mean?

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: You have represented your case in a false light, Doctor, and therefore I am unable to give you my support.

session_3.speaker: Billing
session_3.text: And after what the Mayor was so kind as to tell me just now, I—

session_3.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_3.text: A false light! Leave that part of it to me. Only print my article; I am quite capable of defending it.

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: I am not going to print it. I cannot and will not and dare not print it.

session_3.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_3.text: You dare not? What nonsense!—you are the editor; and an editor controls his paper, I suppose!

session_3.speaker: Aslaksen
session_3.text: No, it is the subscribers, Doctor.

session_3.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_3.text: Fortunately, yes.

session_3.speaker: Aslaksen
session_3.text: It is public opinion—the enlightened public—householders and people of that kind; they control the newspapers.

session_3.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_3.text: And I have all these influences against me?

session_3.speaker: Aslaksen
session_3.text: Yes, you have. It would mean the absolute ruin of the community if your article were to appear.

session_3.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_3.text: Indeed.

session_3.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_3.text: My hat and stick, if you please. Your authority as mayor has come to an untimely end.

session_3.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_3.text: We have not got to the end yet. Then it is quite impossible for you to print my article in the "People's Messenger"?

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Quite impossible—out of regard for your family as well.

session_3.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_3.text: You need not concern yourself about his family, thank you, Mr. Hovstad.

session_3.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_3.text: It will be sufficient, for the guidance of the public, if this appears. It is an official statement. May I trouble you?

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Certainly; I will see that it is printed.

session_3.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_3.text: But not mine. Do you imagine that you can silence me and stifle the truth! You will not find it so easy as you suppose. Mr. Aslaksen, kindly take my manuscript at once and print it as a pamphlet—at my expense. I will have four hundred copies—no, five or six hundred.

session_3.speaker: Aslaksen
session_3.text: If you offered me its weight in gold, I could not lend my press for any such purpose, Doctor. It would be flying in the face of public opinion. You will not get it printed anywhere in the town.

session_3.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_3.text: Then give it me back.

session_3.speaker: Hovstad
session_3.text: Here it is.

session_3.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_3.text: It shall be made public all the same. I will read it out at a mass meeting of the townspeople. All my fellow-citizens shall hear the voice of truth!

session_3.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_3.text: You will not find any public body in the town that will give you the use of their hall for such a purpose.

session_3.speaker: Aslaksen
session_3.text: Not a single one, I am certain.

session_3.speaker: Billing
session_3.text: No, I'm damned if you will find one.

session_3.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_3.text: But this is too shameful! Why should every one turn against you like that?

session_3.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_3.text: I will tell you why. It is because all the men in this town are old women—like you; they all think of nothing but their families, and never of the community.

session_3.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_3.text: Then I will show them that an old woman can be a man for once. I am going to stand by you, Thomas!

session_3.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_3.text: Bravely said, Katherine! It shall be made public—as I am a living soul! If I can't hire a hall, I shall hire a drum, and parade the town with it and read it at every street-corner.

session_3.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_3.text: You are surely not such an errant fool as that!

session_3.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_3.text: Yes, I am.

session_3.speaker: Aslaksen
session_3.text: You won't find a single man in the whole town to go with you.

session_3.speaker: Billing
session_3.text: No, I'm damned if you will.

session_3.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_3.text: Don't give in, Thomas. I will tell the boys to go with you.

session_3.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_3.text: That is a splendid idea!

session_3.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_3.text: Morten will be delighted; and Ejlif will do whatever he does.

session_3.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_3.text: Yes, and Petra!—and you too, Katherine!

session_3.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_3.text: No, I won't do that; but I will stand at the window and watch you, that's what I will do.

session_3.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_3.text: Thank you, my dear! Now you and I are going to try a fall, my fine gentlemen! I am going to see whether a pack of cowards can succeed in gagging a patriot who wants to purify society!

session_3.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_3.text: Now he has sent her out of her senses, too.
